threats.title: Broken onlyOwner/onlyManager Modifiers Enable Complete Protocol Takeover
threats.hypothesis_description: Security.sol modifiers onlyOwner and onlyManager evaluate boolean expressions as no-op statements (`msg.sender == owner;` and `managers[msg.sender] == true;`) without require/revert. Every function guarded by these modifiers is effectively publicly callable by any address. An attacker can call transferOwnership to become owner, setManager to become manager, setProtocolFeePercent to redirect all protocol fees, setFeeRedistributor to a malicious contract, and setERC20Factory to deploy backdoored tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental access control failure affecting all privileged operations across Curves.sol and FeeSplitter.sol. The modifiers compile without error but provide zero protection.

proof_of_signal.pattern: modifier onlyOwner() { msg.sender == owner; _; } — boolean expression as statement with no require/if-revert, compiles as no-op

preconditions: Any external address can call these functions
No additional guards exist in the modifier bodies

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Boolean expression as statement in modifier body
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: msg.sender == owner evaluates to true/false but result is discarded; execution continues to _;

analysis_questions: Can Solidity 0.8.7 compiler warn about unused expression results in modifiers?
Are there any other guards in the call chain that might compensate?
What is the complete list of exploitable functions?
validation_requirements: Call transferOwnership from non-owner address and verify success
Call setProtocolFeePercent from non-owner and verify state change
Verify no compensating controls exist

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls transferOwnership(attacker), then setProtocolFeePercent(1 ether, attacker) to set 100% protocol fee to their address. All subsequent trades route ETH to attacker.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete protocol fund drainage — all future trade fees, ability to deploy malicious factory/redistributor

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying onlyOwner/onlyManager actually revert for unauthorized callers

threats.title: Weaponized onBalanceChange Wipes Victim's Unclaimed Fees Due to Broken Access Control
threats.hypothesis_description: Due to the broken onlyManager modifier (TECH-H001), anyone can call FeeSplitter.onBalanceChange(token, victim). This function sets userFeeOffset[victim] = cumulativeFeePerToken WITHOUT first calling updateFeeCredit to save accumulated fees to unclaimedFees. This permanently destroys the victim's unclaimed fee entitlements.
threats.investigation_rationale: Combines broken access control with the missing updateFeeCredit call in onBalanceChange. An attacker can target specific victims to steal their fee entitlements.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onBalanceChange sets data.userFeeOffset[account] = data.cumulativeFeePerToken without calling updateFeeCredit first; combined with broken onlyManager allowing any caller

preconditions: Victim has accumulated unclaimed fees (cumulativeFeePerToken > userFeeOffset[victim])
onlyManager modifier is a no-op (confirmed in TECH-H001)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Offset overwrite without fee credit preservation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No access control enforcement, no updateFeeCredit call before offset reset

analysis_questions: Can the attacker profit from wiping fees, or is this purely griefing?
Does the wiped fee ETH become claimable by others or permanently stuck?
Can this be combined with TECH-H003 to redirect wiped fees to attacker?
validation_requirements: Call onBalanceChange(token, victim) from non-manager address and verify success
Verify victim's claimable fees decrease to 0 after the call
Check if wiped fees become available to other holders

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Identify victim with large unclaimed fees. 2) Call onBalanceChange(token, victim) from any address (broken modifier). 3) Victim's offset is set to current cumulative without saving owed fees. 4) Victim's accumulated fees are permanently erased.
prerequisites: None — any address can call onBalanceChange

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Targeted destruction of any user's accumulated fees
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any token holder with unclaimed fees
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — fees are permanently lost, not recoverable

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying onBalanceChange preserves accumulated fees or restricts callers

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: onBalanceChange call from non-Curves address detectable

threats.title: DoS on All Trading by Wrapping All Tokens to ERC20 — addFees Reverts on Zero totalSupply
threats.hypothesis_description: FeeSplitter.totalSupply() returns (curvesTokenSupply - curvesTokenBalance[token][address(curves)]) * PRECISION, excluding tokens locked in Curves (wrapped to ERC20). If all circulating tokens are withdrawn to ERC20, totalSupply becomes 0, causing addFees to revert with NoTokenHolders. Since _transferFees calls addFees (when holdersFeePercent > 0), ALL buys and sells revert, completely halting trading for that token.
threats.investigation_rationale: Any token holder can withdraw to ERC20. If enough holders do this (or one whale wraps all but the 1 locked first token), trading halts. The subject always holds the first token, but if even they withdraw, totalSupply can reach 0.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalSupply excludes tokens held by Curves contract; addFees reverts if totalSupply_ == 0; _transferFees calls addFees when holdersFeePercent > 0

preconditions: holdersFeePercent > 0
All internal token holders withdraw to ERC20
Only the 1 locked first-token remains (held by Curves contract, excluded from totalSupply)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: totalSupply can reach 0 when all tokens are wrapped; addFees reverts on 0 supply
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: addFees revert propagates through _transferFees, blocking all buy/sell operations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No fallback handling when totalSupply is 0 in _transferFees

analysis_questions: What is the minimum number of tokens that must be wrapped to trigger DoS?
Can the subject's first token be wrapped?
Is there a recovery path once DoS is triggered?
validation_requirements: Wrap all tokens except first, attempt buy/sell, verify revert
Verify deposit restores functionality

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Subject creates token, buys 1 token. 2) Subject withdraws their 1 token to ERC20. 3) Now curvesTokenBalance[token][curves_contract] = 1, curvesTokenSupply = 1, totalSupply = (1-1)*PRECISION = 0. 4) Any buy/sell of this token reverts because addFees reverts on zero totalSupply.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete trading halt for affected token — users cannot buy or sell
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All traders of the affected token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Possible if someone deposits ERC20 tokens back, restoring totalSupply > 0

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for trading behavior when all tokens are wrapped

threats.title: Withdraw-Deposit Cycle Exploits Stale Fee Offsets for Disproportionate Fee Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: Neither withdraw() nor deposit() call feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange. FeeSplitter reads live balances from Curves. An attacker can: (1) hold tokens with offset C0, (2) withdraw to ERC20 (balance drops to 0 in Curves, FeeSplitter sees 0 balance), (3) wait for fee distributions (cumulativeFeePerToken rises to C1, distributed among fewer holders), (4) deposit back (balance restored to N), (5) claim fees: owed = (C1 - C0) * N, capturing fees from the entire period including when they held 0 internal tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: This exploits the disconnect between live balance reads and stale offsets. The attacker gets credit for fees distributed while they weren't actually holding internal tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw and deposit modify curvesTokenBalance via _transfer but never call feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange; FeeSplitter uses live balance reads with stale offsets

preconditions: ERC20 token is deployed for the subject
Attacker holds curves tokens
Trading continues while attacker's tokens are wrapped (generating holder fees)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: userFeeOffset stays at old value through withdraw/deposit cycle while cumulativeFeePerToken increases
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No onBalanceChange call in withdraw or deposit

analysis_questions: How much fee amplification is possible per cycle?
Can this drain the FeeSplitter below solvency?
Is the attack profitable after gas costs?
validation_requirements: Execute full withdraw-wait-deposit-claim cycle
Compare claimed amount to proportional entitlement
Verify other holders receive less than expected

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Hold 10 tokens, offset at C0. 2) Withdraw all 10 to ERC20. 3) Other trades generate holder fees, C increases by dC. Fees distributed among fewer internal holders. 4) Deposit 10 tokens back. 5) Call claimFees: owed = (C0+dC - C0) * 10 = dC * 10, claiming fees for period when user held 0 internal tokens.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Disproportionate fee extraction — attacker claims fees they didn't earn, depleting pool for legitimate holders

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for fee accounting across withdraw/deposit cycles

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Must own tokens; no additional capital
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-transaction, requires waiting for fee distributions

threats.title: Referral Fee on Sells Permanently Locked When No Referral Defined
threats.hypothesis_description: In _transferFees for sells, sellValue = price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee. When no referral is defined (referralFeeDestination == address(0)), the referral .call is skipped with `(true, bytes(''))`. The referralFee ETH is deducted from the seller's payout but never sent to anyone, permanently locked in the Curves contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Most tokens likely have no referral set. Every sell on these tokens loses referralFee ETH permanently.

proof_of_signal.pattern: When !referralDefined, referral .call is (true, bytes('')), skipping transfer; but sellValue already deducted referralFee

preconditions: referralFeePercent > 0
referralFeeDestination[subject] == address(0)
Sell transaction occurs

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Asymmetric handling of missing referral between buy and sell
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: On buy: referralFee goes to protocol. On sell: referralFee goes nowhere — locked in contract
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No fallback destination for referral fee on sells when referral undefined

analysis_questions: What percentage of tokens have referral destinations set?
What is the cumulative stuck ETH from this over protocol lifetime?
validation_requirements: Sell tokens for a subject with no referral, verify contract balance increase includes referralFee

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not an attack but systematic loss. If referralFeePercent = 2.5% and sell price = 1 ETH, 0.025 ETH per sell is permanently locked. With no extraction mechanism, this accumulates indefinitely.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Cumulative ETH lockup from all sells on tokens without referral destinations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Sellers receive reduced payout; locked ETH is irrecoverable

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying fee routing completeness on sells without referral

threats.title: Excess ETH from Buy Overpayment Permanently Locked in Curves Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: _buyCurvesToken checks `if (msg.value < price + totalFee) revert InsufficientPayment()` but never refunds the difference when msg.value > price + totalFee. Any excess ETH remains permanently locked in the Curves contract with no extraction mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: Users or integrating contracts may send more ETH than required. Without refund logic, overpayments are permanent losses.

proof_of_signal.pattern: msg.value >= price + totalFee check with no refund of excess; all fee transfers use exact amounts, not remaining balance

preconditions: User sends msg.value > price + totalFee
No refund mechanism exists

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Excess msg.value stays in contract permanently
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No refund transfer of (msg.value - price - totalFee) to msg.sender

analysis_questions: Do any integration contracts or frontends send excess ETH?
Is there any admin function to rescue stuck ETH?
validation_requirements: Send excess ETH on buy, verify contract balance increases by excess amount
Verify no mechanism to extract locked ETH

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User estimates buy price but price increases slightly due to concurrent buy. User sends 10% extra for safety. The 10% excess is permanently locked.
prerequisites: User sends more ETH than exact price+fees

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of overpaid ETH per affected transaction
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user or contract that overpays on buy

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for excess ETH handling on buy

threats.title: Any Token Holder Can Deploy ERC20 via withdraw() Bypassing Subject's onlyTokenSubject
threats.hypothesis_description: withdraw() calls _deployERC20 directly when no ERC20 exists, bypassing the onlyTokenSubject modifier on _mint(). Any holder of a curves token can trigger ERC20 deployment with default name/symbol (or whatever metadata was pre-set). Once deployed, ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted prevents the subject from choosing different metadata.
threats.investigation_rationale: This bypasses the subject's exclusive control over ERC20 naming. An attacker could force deployment with default 'Curves'/'CURVES' naming before the subject sets desired name/symbol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw() calls _deployERC20 directly without onlyTokenSubject check; _mint has the modifier but withdraw bypasses _mint

preconditions: Attacker holds at least 1 curves token for the subject
ERC20 not yet deployed for this subject
Subject has not yet set name/symbol (or set them but not yet minted)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Any holder triggers deployment; subject loses ability to choose custom metadata
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No onlyTokenSubject check in withdraw's ERC20 deployment path

analysis_questions: Can the subject change name/symbol after ERC20 deployment?
What metadata is used — pre-set by subject or defaults?
Does this break symbolToSubject uniqueness in any way?
validation_requirements: Buy token as non-subject, call withdraw before subject mints, verify ERC20 deploys with defaults

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Subject creates curve (buys first token). 2) Subject hasn't set name/symbol yet. 3) Attacker buys 1 token. 4) Attacker calls withdraw(subject, 1). 5) ERC20 deploys with 'Curves'/'CURVES' default. 6) Subject cannot change — ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: No direct financial loss, but reputational damage and loss of subject control over token identity

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for ERC20 deployment via withdraw by non-subject

threats.title: FeeSplitter userTokens Array Accumulates Unbounded Duplicates Causing DoS on Batch Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: FeeSplitter.onBalanceChange pushes token to userTokens[account] every time it's called with balance > 0, without checking for duplicates. Since onBalanceChange is called on every buy (and can be called by anyone due to broken onlyManager), the array grows unboundedly with duplicate entries. getUserTokensAndClaimable and batchClaiming iterate this array, eventually hitting gas limits.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unbounded array growth is a known DoS vector. Combined with broken access control, anyone can bloat any user's array by calling onBalanceChange repeatedly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (balanceOf(token, account) > 0) userTokens[account].push(token); — no duplicate check, called on every buy and externally callable

preconditions: User buys tokens multiple times (normal usage) or attacker calls onBalanceChange repeatedly (broken access control)
User later tries to use batchClaiming or getUserTokensAndClaimable

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Array push without duplicate check in frequently-called function
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: userTokens grows O(n) with number of buys, not O(unique tokens)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No duplicate check, no array size limit

analysis_questions: How many duplicates can be added before batchClaiming exceeds block gas limit?
Does batchClaiming double-count fees from duplicates?
Can individual claimFees still work when batchClaiming is DoS'd?
validation_requirements: Call onBalanceChange repeatedly, measure userTokens growth
Attempt batchClaiming with large array, verify gas limit hit
Check if duplicate entries cause double fee claims

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls onBalanceChange(token, victim) thousands of times (free due to broken access control). Victim's userTokens array grows to thousands of entries. Victim's batchClaiming reverts with out-of-gas. Individual claimFees still works but batch functionality is permanently broken.
prerequisites: Broken onlyManager (TECH-H001) for weaponized version; normal usage for organic growth

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Denial of batch claiming functionality; individual claims still possible but more expensive
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user targeted by attacker or heavy traders

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for userTokens array growth and gas limits

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Calculate entries needed to exceed block gas limit
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Gas costs for repeated onBalanceChange calls
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multiple transactions to bloat, then triggered on victim's batch claim
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Large number of onBalanceChange calls for same user detectable

threats.title: FeeSplitter payout via transfer() can permanently block claims for contract wallets, stranding earned ETH
threats.hypothesis_description: FeeSplitter.claimFees and batchClaiming use payable(msg.sender).transfer(...), which forwards only 2300 gas and reverts for many smart contract wallets/multisigs or accounts with complex receive/fallback logic. Such users can become unable to claim earned fees, effectively locking their entitlements.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a common, practical failure mode in modern EVM environments and affects a meaningful portion of users (smart accounts). It does not require adversarial ordering or off-chain steps.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Uses `.transfer(claimable)` and `.transfer(totalClaimable)` which reverts if recipient requires >2300 gas

preconditions: Claimer is a contract address with a non-trivial receive()/fallback() or one that deliberately reverts
Claimer has non-zero claimable fees
FeeSplitter has enough ETH balance to pay

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: ETH payment via transfer with fixed 2300 gas stipend
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: claims revert permanently for certain receivers; no alternative withdrawal method
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no call{value:...} with success check; no pull-to-specified-recipient pattern

analysis_questions: Are intended users expected to be EOAs only, or does protocol target smart accounts?
Is there any alternate claim function that allows specifying a recipient EOA?
What percentage of users on target chain use contract wallets?
validation_requirements: Test claim from a contract wallet with receive() consuming >2300 gas; observe revert
Confirm there is no way to redirect payout to another address
Assess whether batchClaiming aggregates and then transfer() also fails similarly

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not theft: user deploys/uses a smart wallet, accrues fees, then cannot claim because transfer() reverts; fees remain stuck in FeeSplitter until migrated.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users' earned fees can be stranded; moderate aggregate impact depending on adoption
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Low without contract change; user cannot receive via transfer()

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for claiming from smart contract wallets

threats.title: Bonding curve getPrice underflows for supply=0 and amount>1, preventing multi-mint bootstrap and causing unintended DoS for initial buy-with-name/presale setup
threats.hypothesis_description: getPrice uses (supply - 1 + amount) where supply is uint256. When supply=0 and amount>1, (supply - 1) underflows and reverts in Solidity 0.8.7, breaking initial purchases that attempt to buy more than 1 token (e.g., buyCurvesTokenWithName with amount>1).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a deterministic correctness defect that blocks expected protocol flows (bootstrapping supply). While not direct theft, it can break launches and force multiple transactions.
affected_functions: buyCurvesTokenWithName(address curvesTokenSubject, uint256 amount, string memory name, string memory symbol)
buyCurvesTokenForPresale(address curvesTokenSubject, uint256 amount, uint256 startTime, bytes32 merkleRoot, uint256 maxBuy)

inventory_refs.function: buyCurvesTokenWithName(address curvesTokenSubject, uint256 amount, string memory name, string memory symbol)

proof_of_signal.pattern: getPrice computes `supply - 1 + amount` and `2 * (supply - 1 + amount) + 1`; underflows when supply==0 and amount>1

preconditions: Caller attempts initial buy for a new subject with supply=0 and amount>1
Transaction hits getPrice(0, amount) via _buyCurvesToken

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unchecked precondition on uint arithmetic with supply-1
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no branch handling supply==0 for amount>1

analysis_questions: Is protocol design intended to restrict first purchase to amount==1, or is this accidental?
Which user-facing entrypoints allow supply==0 and arbitrary amount (buyCurvesTokenWithName, buyCurvesTokenForPresale)?
Are there UI/workflow mitigations, and do they match docs?
validation_requirements: Test getPrice(0,2) revert
Test buyCurvesTokenWithName(subject,2,...) revert
Confirm whether subjects can still reach desired initial supply via sequential buys

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Griefing/DoS: subjects attempting to initialize with amount>1 will revert repeatedly until they discover the amount==1 limitation.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Limited; primarily operational disruption and potential extra gas cost

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for getPrice at supply=0 and amount>1

threats.title: buy amount=0 allows zero-cost state updates and downstream bloat (ownedCurvesTokenSubjects), enabling gas griefing and storage growth
threats.hypothesis_description: Curves.buyCurvesToken does not enforce amount>0. For supply>0 subjects, getPrice(supply,0) returns 0 and fees are 0, so a caller can execute _buyCurvesToken with msg.value=0, triggering ownership tracking updates and (if holders fees enabled) FeeSplitter hooks. This can be spammed to bloat per-user subject lists and/or FeeSplitter.userTokens via onBalanceChange side effects.
threats.investigation_rationale: Zero-cost callable paths are commonly exploited to create unbounded storage growth or to degrade UX via large arrays. This is practical on L2s with cheap gas.
deployment_assumptions: At least one subject has existing supply > 0 (so non-subject callers pass UnauthorizedCurvesTokenSubject gate)
Optionally, feeRedistributor is enabled (holdersFeePercent>0) to amplify bloat into FeeSplitter.userTokens
threats.config_evidence: Curves.sol _buyCurvesToken requires supply>0 OR msg.sender==subject; _transferFees only calls feeRedistributor when holdersFeePercent>0 and feeRedistributor!=0.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _buyCurvesToken has no `require(amount > 0)`; updates balances/supply by `+= amount` and may call _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject when prior balance was 0

preconditions: Target subject has curvesTokenSupply > 0
Attacker can call buyCurvesToken with amount=0 and msg.value=0
If amplifying into FeeSplitter: holdersFeePercent>0 and feeRedistributor set

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: missing non-zero amount validation enables free calls
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: storage bloat via ownership tracking and potential FeeSplitter.userTokens duplication
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no min amount; no rate limit; no duplicate checks in downstream arrays

analysis_questions: Does _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject add entries when amount=0 and prior balance==0? (it will, because condition checks prior balance using `- amount` logic)
Can this be used to bloat ownedCurvesTokenSubjects for the attacker (and later make transferAllCurvesTokens unusable due to gas)?
If feeRedistributor is enabled, does _transferFees call onBalanceChange even when holderFee is 0, causing FeeSplitter.userTokens bloat at zero cost?
validation_requirements: Test buyCurvesToken(subject,0) succeeds with msg.value=0 when supply>0
Check ownedCurvesTokenSubjects[msg.sender] growth and Transfer/Trade events
If feeRedistributor enabled, check FeeSplitter.userTokens growth and batchClaiming gas

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker repeatedly calls buyCurvesToken(existingSubject,0) to create large per-user arrays, making their own transferAllCurvesTokens expensive/unusable and potentially amplifying FeeSplitter bookkeeping bloat.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect; increased gas costs and potential functional DoS for affected accounts
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Primarily attacker-owned accounts; potentially system-wide if FeeSplitter arrays are amplified and used by UI/batch claims

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests asserting amount>0 on buys or bounding array growth

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: estimate number of spam calls before transferAllCurvesTokens becomes non-viable
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: storage growth visible but may be ignored

threats.title: Unprotected setCurves() Enables Complete FeeSplitter Drain via Malicious Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: FeeSplitter.setCurves() has NO access control modifier whatsoever. An attacker can deploy a malicious contract that returns fabricated balance/supply values, call setCurves() to point to it, then call claimFees() to drain all ETH from the FeeSplitter. The malicious contract returns a huge balance for the attacker (inflating owed fees) and the attacker claims everything.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a standalone critical vulnerability independent of the Security.sol modifier bug. Even if modifiers were fixed, setCurves() would still be callable by anyone.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function setCurves(Curves curves_) public { curves = curves_; } — no modifier, no access check

preconditions: FeeSplitter has ETH balance (fees have been deposited)
At least one token has non-zero cumulativeFeePerToken

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: balanceOf reads from curves contract; attacker controls what curves returns
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deploy contract returning huge curvesTokenBalance for attacker → inflated owed in getClaimableFees
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker claims entire FeeSplitter ETH balance in single transaction

economic_questions: What is the total ETH balance in FeeSplitter at any given time?
Can the attacker also manipulate addFees via the fake curves to maximize extraction?
validation_requirements: Deploy mock contract with inflated balances, call setCurves, then claimFees
Verify no other check prevents claim with fabricated balance

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas for deploying mock + 2 transactions (~0.01 ETH)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When FeeSplitter has accumulated significant fees

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All token holders expecting fee distributions

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if curves reference is validated anywhere after setCurves
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace claimFees with fabricated balance inputs

threats.title: FeeSplitter Insolvency via Transfer-Based Fee Double-Claiming
threats.hypothesis_description: The _transfer function does not notify FeeSplitter (no onBalanceChange call). When tokens are transferred to a fresh address, the recipient's userFeeOffset is 0, allowing them to claim all historical fees. If the sender claims first, then transfers to a fresh address which claims again, the FeeSplitter pays out more than it holds, becoming insolvent and leaving other holders unable to claim.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental accounting flaw: the fee distribution system assumes offsets are synchronized with balance changes, but transfers bypass the synchronization entirely.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _transfer updates balances without calling feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange for sender or receiver

preconditions: Attacker holds curves tokens for a subject with accumulated holdersFees
cumulativeFeePerToken > 0 for the subject token
FeeSplitter has ETH balance

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Recipient offset = 0; owed = cumulativeFeePerToken * balance — claims ALL historical fees
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: 1) Claim fees from address A. 2) Transfer tokens to fresh address B. 3) Claim from B (offset=0 → full historical fees). 4) Repeat with address C, D...
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Each cycle extracts cumulativeFeePerToken * tokenBalance worth of ETH until FeeSplitter is empty

economic_questions: How large is cumulativeFeePerToken after sustained trading activity?
How many cycles before FeeSplitter is fully drained?
Does .transfer() 2300 gas limit prevent re-entrancy during the drain?
validation_requirements: Simulate: claim → transfer → claim cycle and verify double payment
Calculate total extractable vs FeeSplitter balance
Test with multiple subjects/tokens

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost to buy initial tokens on bonding curve + gas
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for claim + transfer per cycle (~0.01 ETH per cycle)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multiple transactions, each cycle drains more

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After significant fee accumulation in FeeSplitter

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All token holders with unclaimed fees
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of claimable fees once FeeSplitter is insolvent

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical cumulativeFeePerToken values and FeeSplitter balance
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace getClaimableFees for fresh address with offset=0

threats.title: Fee Manipulation via Broken Access Control Enables Sell Lockout and Fund Trapping
threats.hypothesis_description: Due to broken onlyManager modifier, anyone can call setMaxFeePercent followed by setExternalFeePercent to set total fees ≥ 100% (1 ether). When totalFee >= price, getSellPriceAfterFee underflows (price - totalFee), and _transferFees computes negative sellValue. Sellers cannot sell tokens, permanently trapping their funds in the bonding curve.
threats.investigation_rationale: This combines the access control vulnerability with fee economics to create an irreversible fund-trapping scenario. Even after the fees are fixed, users who bought at high prices during manipulation may suffer losses.

proof_of_signal.pattern: sellValue = price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee — underflows if total fees >= price

preconditions: Broken onlyManager modifier (confirmed in Security.sol)
maxFeePercent has no hard upper bound vs 1 ether

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: setMaxFeePercent(2 ether) then setExternalFeePercent(0.5 ether, 0.3 ether, 0.3 ether) → fees = 110%+ of price
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Set fees > 100%, all sells revert with underflow, then extort users or wait
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker buys at normal fees, sets fees to >100%, nobody can sell, then resets fees and sells at profit as only seller

economic_questions: Can the attacker also set protocolFeePercent to redirect buy fees to themselves while sells are blocked?
Is there a Solidity 0.8.7 checked arithmetic revert in _transferFees for the underflow?
validation_requirements: Set fees above 100% and confirm sell reverts
Verify no other bounds check prevents this configuration

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All tokens become unsellable — total bonding curve TVL at risk
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Infinite if combined with fee destination redirection

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All token holders across all curves

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm sell path reverts when totalFee > price

threats.title: Unbacked ERC20 Minting via Broken Access Control on Factory Setter
threats.hypothesis_description: Broken access control allows anyone to replace the ERC20 factory, enabling the deployment of malicious ERC20 tokens that break the 1:1 backing invariant.
threats.investigation_rationale: The onlyOwner modifier in Security.sol is a no-op. Any user can call setERC20Factory to inject a malicious factory contract. When users withdraw internal tokens, the malicious factory deploys an ERC20 token that grants the attacker infinite minting rights, breaking the protocol's core solvency invariant.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setERC20Factory protected by broken onlyOwner modifier

preconditions: Attacker deploys a malicious CurvesERC20Factory contract

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: ERC20 totalSupply diverges from internal locked tokens
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls setERC20Factory, then triggers withdraw to deploy trojan token
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker mints infinite ERC20 tokens and dumps them on external DEX liquidity pools

economic_questions: How quickly can an attacker establish external liquidity for the fake tokens?
Does the malicious token affect internal bonding curve pricing?
validation_requirements: Deploy malicious factory, call setERC20Factory, trigger withdraw, and mint tokens
Verify 1:1 peg is broken

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on external DEX liquidity for the exported tokens

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires external DEX pairs (e.g., Token/ETH) to extract value
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after a popular subject launches
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete destruction of token value on external markets

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All external liquidity for the affected token

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify onlyOwner modifier evaluation behavior in Solidity 0.8.7

threats.title: ERC20 Symbol Squatting via Global Registry
threats.hypothesis_description: The global symbol registry allows any user to permanently claim valuable ERC20 symbols by creating a cheap curve, enabling extortion of legitimate projects.
threats.investigation_rationale: The symbolToSubject mapping enforces global uniqueness for ERC20 symbols across the entire protocol. An attacker can buy 1 token (costing 0 ETH + fees) to initialize a curve, then call setNameAndSymbol to claim highly desirable symbols (e.g., 'USDC', 'YGG', or influencer names). Legitimate subjects are permanently blocked from using their own branding.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (symbolToSubject[symbol] != address(0)) revert InvalidERC20Metadata();

preconditions: Attacker identifies a valuable symbol before the legitimate subject claims it

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Global namespace with near-zero cost to claim
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker creates dummy curves to squat symbols
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker demands ransom from legitimate projects to transfer the subject address (if possible) or launches fake tokens to scam buyers

economic_questions: Can the attacker transfer the curve ownership to sell the squatted symbol?
Does this prevent the legitimate subject from launching entirely?
validation_requirements: Claim a symbol with Address A
Attempt to claim the same symbol with Address B and verify revert

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Right before a major influencer or project announces their token
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Severe brand damage and potential phishing risks for users

threats.title: Protocol Fee and Referral Fee Permanently Locked in Curves Contract on Sells
threats.hypothesis_description: In _transferFees during sells, the sellValue sent to the seller is computed as price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee. The protocolFee is deducted from the seller's payout but never forwarded to protocolFeeDestination (only the sellValue goes to firstDestination which is msg.sender on sells). Similarly, when no referral is defined, the referralFee is deducted from sellValue but the transfer is skipped (returns (true, bytes(''))), permanently locking both amounts in the Curves contract. There is no withdrawal function for this locked ETH.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an accounting error in the sell fee distribution. ETH accumulates permanently in the Curves contract with no extraction mechanism, representing a continual loss.

proof_of_signal.pattern: For sells: firstDestination=msg.sender, sellValue=price-protocolFee-subjectFee-referralFee-holderFee sent to seller. protocolFee is deducted but never sent anywhere on sells. referralFee skipped if no referral defined.

preconditions: protocolFeePercent > 0 (protocol fee is configured)
Any sell transaction occurs

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: On buy: protocolFee sent to protocolFeeDestination. On sell: protocolFee deducted from seller but never forwarded — asymmetric handling
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — happens automatically on every sell

operational_questions: How much ETH has been locked from sells since deployment?
Is there any admin function to extract locked ETH from Curves?
Does this affect contract solvency for future sells?
validation_requirements: Execute a sell, trace ETH flow, verify protocolFee stays in contract
Verify no receive/fallback/withdraw function in Curves for extracting trapped ETH
Calculate cumulative ETH loss over protocol lifetime

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not directly exploitable for profit, but causes permanent ETH leakage
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Over time, Curves contract accumulates ETH from all sell protocolFees
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Eventually, contract may have insufficient ETH for sell payouts if bonding curve backing erodes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: No recovery — ETH is permanently locked with no extraction function
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — systemic issue on every sell

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Continuous ETH leakage; protocol loses revenue from all sells; potential long-term insolvency
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All sell operations, protocol fee collection

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Curves contract ETH balance growing beyond expected bonding curve reserves
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Protocol fee destination not receiving fees from sells
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Increasing contract balance discrepancy over time

threats.title: Denial of Service Window at Exact Presale Start Time
threats.hypothesis_description: There is a logical gap in the presale timing checks. `buyCurvesToken` reverts if `startTime >= block.timestamp`, and `buyCurvesTokenWhitelisted` reverts if `startTime <= block.timestamp`. At the exact block where `startTime == block.timestamp`, neither function can be called.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates a 1-block denial of service window where trading is completely halted. While brief, it breaks the invariant that trading should transition smoothly from presale to public sale.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Mutually exclusive boundary conditions (`>=` and `<=`) creating a dead zone

preconditions: Presale is configured with a specific `startTime`
A transaction is mined in the exact block where `block.timestamp == startTime`

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: When `startTime == block.timestamp`, both `startTime >= block.timestamp` and `startTime <= block.timestamp` evaluate to true, causing both functions to revert.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: One of the checks should be strictly `<` or `>`

analysis_questions: Do both functions revert at `startTime == block.timestamp`?
Can this be exploited for MEV or griefing?
validation_requirements: Set `startTime` to a specific timestamp
Warp block timestamp to exactly `startTime`
Attempt to call both buy functions and verify they revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Users attempting to snipe the public launch at the exact `startTime` will have their transactions revert. This causes confusion and wasted gas, and delays the actual launch by one block.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Wasted gas for users, temporary trading halt
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Resolves automatically in the next block

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Spike in reverted transactions at launch time

threats.title: Presale Whitelist Manipulation - Subject Can Change Merkle Root During Active Presale
threats.hypothesis_description: setWhitelist allows the subject to change the merkle root when supply <= 1. Since the subject buys exactly 1 token during buyCurvesTokenForPresale (supply becomes 1), they can call setWhitelist during an active presale to change the whitelist, excluding already-whitelisted users or adding new users. Existing presale participants who planned to buy more tokens find their proofs invalidated.
threats.investigation_rationale: setWhitelist checks supply > 1 to revert, meaning supply=0 or supply=1 is allowed. After buyCurvesTokenForPresale sets supply=1, the subject can modify merkleRoot freely during the presale phase, undermining trust in the whitelist commitment.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setWhitelist: if (supply > 1) revert CurveAlreadyExists(); allows modification when supply == 1 (during active presale)

preconditions: Subject has created a presale via buyCurvesTokenForPresale (supply=1)
Presale is active (block.timestamp < startTime)
Whitelisted users exist who plan to buy

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: supply == 1 after presale creation; setWhitelist only blocks supply > 1
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Subject changes merkle root to exclude certain users or include favored parties after seeing demand
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Subject can manipulate who gets early access, potentially creating unfair advantage for insiders

economic_questions: Can the subject extract economic value by selectively modifying the whitelist?
Can the subject set merkleRoot to bytes32(0) to effectively disable the whitelist?
How does this interact with presalesBuys tracking?
validation_requirements: Verify setWhitelist succeeds when supply=1 during active presale
Test if merkleRoot=0 allows any proof to pass
Confirm whitelist changes take effect immediately

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of initial token purchase (first token is free)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect value from controlling presale access

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During active presale after seeing demand

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of presale access / early pricing advantage

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Presale timing mechanics, merkle root validation edge cases
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test setWhitelist at supply=1 during presale

threats.title: Unbounded ownedCurvesTokenSubjects Array Growth Causes transferAllCurvesTokens Gas DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: ownedCurvesTokenSubjects array never removes entries even when balance reaches 0 after selling. An attacker can inflate a victim's array by transferring 1 token from many different subjects to the victim's address. Each transfer calls _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject which adds the subject (with O(n) duplicate check). When the victim calls transferAllCurvesTokens, the unbounded iteration causes out-of-gas.
threats.investigation_rationale: _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject (novel) iterates the entire array to check for duplicates, and the array never shrinks. transferAllCurvesTokens (novel) iterates the entire array to transfer all tokens. Both become gas-prohibitive as the array grows.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject iterates full array for duplicate check, never removes entries; array grows monotonically

preconditions: Many curve subjects exist in the protocol
Attacker can buy tokens in multiple subjects and transfer 1 to victim
Victim relies on transferAllCurvesTokens for migration

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject: O(n) duplicate check per addition; transferAllCurvesTokens: O(n) iteration with _transfer per entry
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Transfer 1 token from 1000 different subjects to victim → array length 1000 → gas cost ~30M+ for transferAllCurvesTokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack - victim cannot use transferAllCurvesTokens for account migration

economic_questions: What is the cost to buy minimum tokens in N subjects?
At what array size does transferAllCurvesTokens exceed block gas limit?
Can victim still transfer individual tokens as fallback?
validation_requirements: Estimate gas per iteration in transferAllCurvesTokens
Calculate block gas limit threshold for array size
Verify entries are never removed on sell/transfer-away

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost to buy 1 token in each of N subjects (bonding curve cost at supply+1)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: ETH for multiple minimum token purchases

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Convenience loss - individual transfers still work

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Gas cost per array iteration, block gas limit
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate array growth to DoS threshold

threats.title: setNameAndSymbol Does Not Register Symbol - Parallel Subjects Race to Deploy Same Symbol
threats.hypothesis_description: setNameAndSymbol stores name/symbol in externalCurvesTokens but does NOT register the symbol in symbolToSubject. Multiple subjects can set the same symbol simultaneously. Only the first to deploy ERC20 (via mint or withdraw) gets the symbol registered. The second deployment reverts with InvalidERC20Metadata, potentially after the subject has already promoted their token under that symbol.
threats.investigation_rationale: setNameAndSymbol (novel) only checks if symbolToSubject[symbol] != address(0) (no one deployed it yet) but doesn't register the symbol. symbolToSubject is only updated in _deployERC20. This creates a race condition between subjects using the same symbol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setNameAndSymbol checks symbolToSubject[symbol] != address(0) but does NOT set symbolToSubject[symbol] = curvesTokenSubject, allowing concurrent reservations

preconditions: Two or more subjects desire the same symbol
Neither has deployed the ERC20 yet

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: symbolToSubject only updated in _deployERC20, not in setNameAndSymbol
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Subject A and B both set symbol 'POPULAR'. First to mint/withdraw deploys successfully. Second's mint/withdraw reverts.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Subject B loses ability to use their promoted symbol, potentially after marketing it to users

economic_questions: How often would symbol collisions occur in practice?
Can a subject recover by setting a different symbol after collision?
Does the collision cause permanent damage or just inconvenience?
validation_requirements: Verify two subjects can setNameAndSymbol with same symbol
Confirm second deployment reverts
Test if affected subject can call setNameAndSymbol again with different symbol

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost to create own curve (first token is free)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When a popular subject announces their planned symbol
